text.title: Solo SaaS Legal Docs Pack Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for the minimum legal-doc pack a solo SaaS must publish before activating billing.

rule.statement: Five documents MUST be live at stable URLs before the first paid charge: (1) Terms of Service, (2) Privacy Policy, (3) Refund / Cancellation Policy, (4) Cookie Banner + Cookie Policy, (5) basic Data Processing Addendum available on request. Missing any one of the five blocks billing activation.
rule.rationale: Stripe / Paddle risk reviews and GDPR complaints both check for this minimum set. Shipping with 4 of 5 in place is functionally the same as shipping with 0 — the missing one is the one auditors find.

rule.statement: Every document is based on a named, dated source (e.g., "Iubenda generator 2026-04", "Stripe Atlas ToS 2025-12") AND edited to insert: legal entity name, jurisdiction, billing platform name, support email, refund-window in days. Forbidden: pasting a template with `[COMPANY NAME]` placeholders unedited.
rule.rationale: Unedited placeholders signal "no one reviewed this" to risk teams and regulators. The edit pass forces the founder to confirm each clause applies to their actual product.

rule.statement: The billing platform's checkout flow MUST link to the live ToS + Privacy URLs from the checkout page, and the founder MUST verify these URLs return HTTP 200 from a fresh incognito session before flipping billing live. A CI / pre-launch checklist enforces the URL check.
rule.rationale: 404s on legal URLs are a common Stripe risk-review trigger and a self-inflicted GDPR exposure. A pre-flight URL check catches the regression after the third deploy renames the page slug.

rule.statement: Each document names exactly one governing jurisdiction (state / country) and one dispute-resolution mechanism. "International", "where applicable", or unfilled placeholders are rejected. The jurisdiction matches the legal entity's place of incorporation, NOT the founder's residence if different.
rule.rationale: Unspecified jurisdiction defaults to wherever the customer sues, eliminating the legal-entity protection. Named jurisdiction is what makes the entity wrapper meaningful.

rule.statement: Each document lists a single contact email (`legal@` or `support@` on the product's own domain) that is monitored daily. Personal Gmail addresses are forbidden; aliases that bounce or auto-reply with "we got it" without human follow-up are forbidden.
rule.rationale: GDPR data-subject requests, DMCA notices, and Stripe risk replies arrive at the legal contact. Unmonitored addresses convert response-time SLAs into automatic violations.

example.code: LEGAL-DOC PACK STATUS (pre-launch gate) - ToS https://app.example.com/legal/terms [200 OK] v2026-05-12 - Privacy Policy https://app.example.com/legal/privacy [200 OK] v2026-05-12 - Refund Policy https://app.example.com/legal/refunds [200 OK] v2026-05-12 - Cookie Policy https://app.example.com/legal/cookies [200 OK] v2026-05-12 - DPA (on request) legal@example.com (template 2026-05-12) v2026-05-12 Entity: Example Labs Ltd, registered Estonia (15998877) Jurisdiction: Estonia, ICC arbitration Contact: legal@example.com (monitored, daily) Billing flip: BLOCKED until all 5 rows = OK
example.note: Five docs, fresh-cache URL check, named entity + jurisdiction, monitored contact. Billing activation depends on this status block.

antipattern.description: Founder copies a Notion ToS template, leaves "Last updated: [DATE]" and "[COMPANY NAME]" untouched, links it from the footer, and goes live.
antipattern.reason: Violates r2 (unedited placeholders) and r4 (no jurisdiction). Stripe risk-review will flag within 30 days; GDPR exposure unbounded.
